title: The When and Why of Abandonment: The Role of Organizational Differences in Medical Technology Life Cycles

dataset_schema.label: AHCA Hospital Discharge Dataset
dataset_schema.description: Patient demographics, ICD codes, DRG discharge codes, admission/discharge dates, hospital identifiers, physician identifiers, payer type.

dataset_schema.label: Hospital Organisational Dataset
dataset_schema.description: Hospital identity, ownership classification (for-profit, non-profit, academic medical centre), location.

original_intent.label: AHCA
original_intent.description: Billing, compliance, reimbursement tracking, and policy evaluation.

original_intent.label: Hospital Organisational
original_intent.description: Classify health systems and analyse institutional incentives and regulatory structures.

repurposed_use: Linked datasets to analyse how organisational incentives influence adoption and abandonment of medical technologies over time.

limitations.label: Construct Validity
limitations.description: Hospital type used as proxy for incentives and culture, oversimplifying organisational differences.

limitations.label: Temporal Instability
limitations.description: Hospital characteristics change over time but are treated as static classifications.

limitations.label: Granularity
limitations.description: Mismatch between granular patient-level data and high-level hospital organisational data.

transformations.label: Temporal Structuring
transformations.description: Convert cross-sectional data into longitudinal time series aligned with technology introduction periods.

transformations.label: Data Integration
transformations.description: Link patient, hospital, and technology datasets.

transformations.label: Feature Engineering
transformations.description: Derive adoption and abandonment variables from procedure codes.

transformations.label: Aggregation
transformations.description: Aggregate patient-level data to hospital-level metrics (adoption rate, abandonment rate).

notes: Illustrates reinterpretation of billing codes as behavioural signals of technology adoption and shift in unit of analysis from patient to organisation.
